records.ety: [OF. conveir, convoier, to escort, convoy, F. convoyer, LL. conviare, fr. L. con- + via way. See Viaduct, Voyage, and cf. Convoy.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Conveyed (kn*vād"); p. pr. & vb. n. Conveying.]

defs: To carry from one place to another; to bear or transport.
quotes: I will convey them by sea in floats.
Convey me to my bed, then to my grave.

defs: To cause to pass from one place or person to another; to serve as a medium in carrying (anything) from one place or person to another; to transmit; as, air conveys sound; words convey ideas.
To transfer or deliver to another; to make over, as property; more strictly (Law), to transfer (real estate) or pass (a title to real estate) by a sealed writing.
quotes: The Earl of Desmond . . . secretly conveyed all his lands to feoffees in trust.

defs: To impart or communicate; as, to convey an impression; to convey information.
quotes: Men fill one another's heads with noise and sound, but convey not thereby their thoughts.

defs: To manage with privacy; to carry out.
quotes: I . . . will convey the business as I shall find means.

defs: To carry or take away secretly; to steal; to thieve.
To play the thief; to steal.
quotes: But as I am Crack, I will convey, crossbite, and cheat upon Simplicius.
